aliases: Icy Coconut Kopi 冰感椰咖啡

romanized: Icy Coconut Kopi 冰感椰咖啡

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for iced coconut coffee. FOODON_03301099 (coffee beverage) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Coffee and coffee substitutes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for coconut-flavoured iced coffee in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 (Coffee) is the closest parent concept. No specific SNOMED CT code exists for iced coconut coffee as a prepared beverage.

preparation_method: blended with coconut milk or coconut cream
allergens: milk (condensed milk, coconut milk may be processed in facilities with dairy)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Iced Coconut Coffee is approximately 300 g (one large cup), as typically served at Singaporean kopitiams and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: Kopi (Singapore coffee with condensed milk) and coconut milk, adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data for kopi and USDA FoodData Central coconut milk values
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for iced coconut coffee as a composite beverage. Values are estimated by combining HPB Singapore Foodo data for traditional kopi (coffee with sweetened condensed milk, ~50 kcal/100 ml) with USDA FoodData Central data for canned coconut milk (USDA FDC ID 170172, ~197 kcal/100 g, fat 21.3 g/100 g). The final blend is modelled as approximately 60% kopi base and 40% coconut milk by volume, yielding a composite per-100 g estimate. Sugar contribution from condensed milk and any added sugar is included. Values carry medium confidence due to recipe variability across vendors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous beverage: sweetened coffee with milk (GI ~40–50) and coconut milk (GI ~41), sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed GI table
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for iced coconut coffee. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on: (1) University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened coffee with milk (GI ~40–50); (2) coconut milk GI of approximately 41 per Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 31.5) / 100 = 14.2, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 31.5 g.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit iced coconut coffee to one serving per day, as the caffeine from robusta kopi may cause transient blood pressure elevation, and the sodium content (~84 mg per 300 g serving) contributes to daily sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content from condensed milk and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit iced coconut coffee, as one 300 g serving delivers approximately 29 g of sugar (predominantly from sweetened condensed milk), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; unsweetened or reduced-sugar alternatives are strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume iced coconut coffee in moderation, as coconut milk contributes approximately 15 g of saturated fat per 300 g serving, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Coconut Coffee (冰感椰咖啡, also known as Icy Coconut Kopi) is a cold Singaporean coffee beverage that combines traditional kopi — strong robusta coffee brewed with a sock filter and typically sweetened with condensed milk — with coconut milk or coconut cream, served over ice. The drink has a rich, creamy texture with a pronounced coconut aroma layered over the bold, slightly bitter notes of robusta coffee, balanced by the sweetness of condensed milk. It is a modern fusion variant of the classic Singaporean kopitiam coffee culture, adapted to appeal to consumers seeking tropical flavour profiles. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 285 kcal, 17 g of fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut milk), and around 29 g of sugar. The high saturated fat and sugar content make it a moderate-to-high concern beverage for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, Type 2 Diabetes, or weight. The caffeine content from robusta kopi (typically 150–200 mg per serving) is relevant for individuals with hypertension or caffeine sensitivity. The beverage is generally halal-compliant and gluten-free but is not suitable for vegans due to condensed milk. It is commonly found at Singaporean kopitiams, hawker centres, and specialty coffee stalls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in iced coconut coffee?
Is iced coconut kopi suitable for diabetics?
What is the sugar content of coconut kopi?
Is iced coconut coffee high in saturated fat?
Can I drink coconut kopi if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for iced coconut coffee as a named composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Singapore Foodo kopi data with USDA FoodData Central coconut milk data. GI is estimated from analogous beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability across vendors and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis (HPB Singapore Foodo kopi + USDA FDC coconut milk) and carry medium confidence. Vendor recipes vary significantly; the Hai Kah Lang Funan Mall version may differ from this estimate. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 3. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 4. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 5. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 7. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 9. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 10. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 11. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 12. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 13. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as coconut milk and kopi do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero by formulation. 14. glycemic_index set to 45 (low category) as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sweetened coffee with milk and coconut milk GI data; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific beverage. 15. GrabFood source URL and restaurant data (Hai Kah Lang - Funan Mall) are preserved from the input seed record for provenance traceability but are not used as primary nutrient evidence per Project Veritas source policy.
